children.name: Upupa
children.description: One of the most striking birds of the Western Palearctic — salmon-pink with bold black-and-white barred wings and a dramatic fan-shaped crest raised in alarm or display. The oop-oop-oop call carries far over open Swedish landscapes. An irregular visitor to the south, with occasional breeding records in warm years.

children.name: Upupa epops
children.description: An unmistakeable pinkish-brown bird with bold black-and-white wings and a fan-shaped crest raised in excitement. A scarce but regular visitor to southern Sweden, probing lawns and bare ground for insect larvae.

children.name: Upupa africana
children.description: Long treated as a subspecies of the Eurasian Hoopoe, the African Hoopoe is now widely recognised as a distinct species based on plumage and genetic differences. It is deeper rufous-orange than its Eurasian counterpart, with broader black-and-white wing bars. Found across sub-Saharan Africa from the Sahel south to the Cape, it inhabits open woodland, savanna, and farmland where it forages for beetles, larvae, and other invertebrates with its long, curved bill. The species is common throughout its range and not considered threatened.

children.name: Upupa marginata
children.description: Endemic to Madagascar, this island hoopoe was long classed as a race of the Eurasian Hoopoe but is now treated as a full species. It resembles the Eurasian Hoopoe in general pattern but has a distinctive paler, more washed-out colouration and occupies a range of habitats from spiny forest in the south to humid eastern forests. It is the only hoopoe on the island and is a common and conspicuous bird in Malagasy villages and open countryside, frequently seen probing lawns and earth for invertebrate prey.
